channel.title: Nature
channel.description: Nature is the foremost international weekly scientific journal in the world and is the flagship journal for Nature Portfolio. It publishes the finest peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature publishes landmark papers, award winning news, leading comment and expert opinion on important, topical scientific news and events that enable readers to share the latest discoveries in science and evolve the discussion amongst the global scientific community.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: [6] Mesoscale atomic engineering in a crystal lattice
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 13 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10431-9

Electron-beam control enables deterministic placement of tens of thousands of atomic defects in three-dimensional crystals, creating stable, programmable artificial matter for scalable quantum and nanoscale technologies.
item.dc:title: [6] Mesoscale atomic engineering in a crystal lattice
item.dc:creator: Julian Klein, Kevin M. Roccapriore, Mads Weile, et al.
Kevin M. Roccapriore
Mads Weile
Sergii Grytsiuk
Andrew R. Lupini
Zdenek Sofer
Dimitar Pashov
Mark van Schilfgaarde
Swagata Acharya
Malte Rösner
Frances M. Ross
item.description:
Last authors: Malte Rösner, Frances M. Ross

Authors: Julian Klein, Kevin M. Roccapriore, Mads Weile, et al.

Score 6/10 C_MAYBE

Why: Nanoscale methodology for deterministic placement of atomic defects in crystals.

Tags: #instrumentation #nanotechnology #quantum-materials

Abstract: Nature, Published online: 13 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10431-9 Electron-beam control enables deterministic placement of tens of thousands of atomic defects in three-dimensional crystals, creating stable, programmable artificial matter for scalable quantum and nanoscale technologies.

item.title: [6] Targeted electron beam creates thousands of atomic crystal defects
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 13 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01328-8

An electron-beam technique that can precisely create thousands of atomic defects in a crystal could be used to build quantum devices.
item.dc:title: [6] Targeted electron beam creates thousands of atomic crystal defects
item.dc:creator: Toma Susi
item.description:
Last authors: Toma Susi

Score 6/10 C_MAYBE

Why: Methodology for creating precise crystal defects for quantum devices, relevant for material modification.

Tags: #instrumentation #defects #quantummaterials

Abstract: Nature, Published online: 13 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01328-8 An electron-beam technique that can precisely create thousands of atomic defects in a crystal could be used to build quantum devices.
